metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `deletion-impact` to the `OVERLAY_IDS` tuple in `registry.py` and add corresponding `{prompt: code-review-deletion-impact, when: ${{ ... }}}` lines to both the `round_a` and `round_b` batch steps in `gates/code-review.yaml` — the existing dispatch loop only iterates `OVERLAY_IDS` members.
findings.finding: The plan's scope lists `criteria_routing.json entry` as the registration step for the new `deletion-impact` overlay, but the actual overlay dispatch machinery requires the overlay ID to be added to the closed `OVERLAY_IDS` tuple in `registry.py`. `overlay_union` iterates only `OVERLAY_IDS` (line 87/91 of workflow_ops.py); an ID absent from that tuple can never appear in `include_<overlay>` flag outputs, never reach a round_a or round_b batch `when:` guard, and never be triggered regardless of content-trigger or routing-entry. The plan makes no mention of updating `OVERLAY_IDS`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'criteria_routing.json entry'; src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py:34-46 (OVERLAY_IDS tuple); src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:87,91
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py`, append `"deletion-impact"` to `OVERLAY_IDS`. In `gates/code-review.yaml`, add `- { prompt: code-review-deletion-impact, when: "${{ steps.triggers.outputs.include_deletion_impact }}" }` under `round_a.batch.criteria` and the analogous `union` reference under `round_b.batch.criteria`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `deletion-impact` to `OVERLAY_IDS` in `registry.py` (the single source of truth for the overlay vocabulary) as an explicit edit-target in the plan's Scope section.
findings.finding: `deletion-impact` is not in the closed `OVERLAY_IDS` tuple in `registry.py`, yet the plan's scope omits adding it there. Every dispatch path — `glob_triggered_overlays`, `overlay_union` in `workflow_ops.py`, both `round_a`/`round_b` batch steps in `code-review.yaml`, `overlay_flag_key`, and the `overlay_union_output` schema — derives exclusively from `OVERLAY_IDS`. A `criteria_routing.json` entry alone cannot activate a new overlay.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py:34 (`OVERLAY_IDS` tuple)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"deletion-impact"` to the `OVERLAY_IDS` tuple in `registry.py` and list `registry.py:OVERLAY_IDS` explicitly in the Scope alongside `criteria_routing.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py` (the `overlay_union` step, to accept and union a `content_triggered` set) and the `round_a`/`round_b` batch entries in `gates/code-review.yaml` as explicit edit targets in the plan's Scope.
findings.finding: `workflow_ops.py::overlay_union` and both `round_a`/`round_b` batch steps in `code-review.yaml` are unenumerated consumers that require edits to wire the content-triggered overlay. `overlay_union` currently unions only `glob_set` and `recommend_ids`; it has no parameter or code path for a content-predicate set. The `round_a`/`round_b` steps hardcode one `when:` entry per overlay id.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:74-96 / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml:69-111

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add `'deletion-impact'` to the `OVERLAY_IDS` tuple in `registry.py` and update the no-drift guard test in `test_code_review_recommend_overlays.py` to include it.
findings.finding: 'deletion-impact' is named as the new overlay id throughout the plan but is absent from the closed OVERLAY_IDS tuple in registry.py. The plan specifies adding a 'criteria_routing.json entry' but never explicitly calls out adding the id to OVERLAY_IDS — yet filter_recommend_overlays silently drops any id not in OVERLAY_IDS, so the overlay can never be escalated to by the base reviewer, and glob_triggered_overlays iterates only OVERLAY_IDS, so glob-triggers would also silently miss it.
findings.location: Scope section ('criteria_routing.json entry') / src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py:34 (OVERLAY_IDS tuple)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"deletion-impact"` to `OVERLAY_IDS` in `registry.py:34-46`. Update `test_code_review_recommend_overlays.py` to assert the new id is present. Specify the `criteria_routing.json` entry fields: `exec: "AGENT"` (or a new `"CONTENT"` exec mode if the content-trigger is its own class), `applies_to: []` (content-trigger only, no glob), `default_posture: "advisory"`, `block_threshold: 0.95`, `blocking_enabled: false`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the content-trigger seam API: specify the function signature (e.g., `content_triggered_overlays(diff_text: str) -> list[str]`), the predicate registration protocol (analogous to `applies_to` globs), and how the result joins the `overlay_union` formula — then update `code-review.yaml` to thread `diff_text` into the triggers step.
findings.finding: The content-triggered overlay seam is referenced but never structurally specified: the plan names 'registry.glob_triggered_overlays seam' as the extension point for content triggers, yet the API shape (function signature, predicate protocol, return type, how content-trigger results join the overlay_union formula) is fully absent. The existing overlay_union formula is `(glob ∪ recommend) − already_run`; a third operand 'content' has no placement.
findings.location: What section ('deterministic diff-content predicate that activates an overlay, alongside today's path-glob triggers (registry.glob_triggered_overlays seam)') / src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:overlay_union / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml:triggers step

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete recall-eval fixture: a small corpus of diffs (true-positive removals, rename-with-callers-updated, unrelated diffs) with explicit pass/fail thresholds, so AC line 3 is verifiable before merge.
findings.finding: The polyglot regex trigger heuristic and the LLM one-hop caller search are asserted without an empirical validation plan: the AC names a 'recall eval' but specifies no fixture corpus, no false-positive threshold, and no success metric, leaving the trigger precision/recall unvalidated before build-out.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Dangling-deletion recall eval passes; a valid rename-with-callers-updated does not fire.'
findings.suggested_fix: Create a pytest fixture under tests/rebar/llm/code_review/ with ≥5 representative diff samples (def removal, class removal, rename-with-callers-updated, non-def removal, comment-only removal) and assert trigger fires/no-fires correctly on each, plus define a minimum recall threshold (e.g. ≥90%) for the recall eval.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the data-flow path for `diff_text` from `assemble_diff` to the content-trigger predicate: either add `diff_text` as an output of the `assemble_diff` step and a new input to `overlay_union` (with schema + YAML updates), or introduce a dedicated content-trigger scripted step in the gate YAML that receives `diff_text`.
findings.finding: The content-trigger predicate (regex over '-' diff lines) must consume raw `diff_text`, but the plan does not specify how `diff_text` reaches the trigger seam. `assemble_diff` currently outputs only `{context, changed_files}` — not `diff_text` as a separate field — and `overlay_union` currently takes only `changed_files` as its trigger input. Placing the content-trigger 'alongside glob_triggered_overlays in registry.py' without wiring `diff_text` through the YAML/schema leaves the mechanism incomplete: the seam function has no data to operate on at the point it would be called.
findings.location: What section / Scope: registry.py trigger seam / gates/code-review.yaml wiring
findings.suggested_fix: Add `diff_text: dc.diff_text` to assemble_diff's return dict; add `diff_text` to overlay_union_input.schema.json; thread it via `diff_text: ${{ steps.assemble_diff.outputs.diff_text }}` in both `triggers` and `union` steps in code-review.yaml; pass it to `registry.content_triggered_overlays(diff_text)` inside `overlay_union()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add acceptance criteria covering (a) gates/code-review.yaml wires the new content-trigger to the deletion-impact overlay, (b) code-review-deletion-impact.md exists and contains the required reviewer prompt structure, and (c) criteria_routing.json entry routes the overlay's findings correctly.
findings.finding: Three scoped deliverables — gates/code-review.yaml wiring, code-review-deletion-impact.md (the reviewer prompt), and criteria_routing.json entry — are listed under Scope but have no corresponding acceptance criterion. A reviewer implementing or verifying the plan cannot confirm these artifacts are correct, complete, or even present.
findings.location: Scope section; Acceptance Criteria section
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] gates/code-review.yaml registers the deletion-impact overlay under the content-trigger seam and an integration smoke-test confirms it activates end-to-end.' and '- [ ] code-review-deletion-impact.md exists with the agentic one-hop caller-search prompt and is referenced by criteria_routing.json.' These directly map to the three uncovered Scope items.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where the polyglot trigger regex lives and whether it is configurable (e.g., a field in gates/code-review.yaml or registry.py), so adding language patterns doesn't require a code change.
findings.finding: The polyglot regex that detects removed def/class/signature lines is a rule expected to evolve (new languages, edge cases, decorator patterns) but the plan does not specify whether it is configurable or hardcoded, and where it lives.
findings.location: What section — 'triggered when the diff removes a def/class/signature (polyglot regex over ''-'' lines)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the content-triggered overlay contract: what a trigger predicate must do, how it composes with path-glob triggers, and the rationale for the deterministic-predicate approach.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (content-triggered overlays alongside path-glob triggers) but includes no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry capturing the decision rationale, the contract for the new seam, or guidance for future overlay authors.
findings.location: Scope section — 'registry.py (trigger seam)' and 'What' section describing content-triggered overlays as a new capability

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope: update `tests/unit/test_code_review_recommend_overlays.py` (closed-set and no-drift tests), `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py` (routing coverage test), and `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-base.md` (overlay catalog bullet) as required edit targets when `deletion-impact` is added to `OVERLAY_IDS`.
findings.finding: Four existing tests enforce the closed-set and no-drift invariants of `OVERLAY_IDS` and will fail when `deletion-impact` is added, but none are enumerated as edit targets in the plan's scope.
findings.location: Scope section / tests/unit/test_code_review_recommend_overlays.py / tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define AC3 with a concrete test corpus (e.g., N synthetic diffs covering at least: pure deletion, rename-with-update, signature change, unrelated diff), a minimum recall/precision threshold, and the command that runs the eval (e.g., `pytest tests/test_deletion_trigger.py`).
findings.finding: AC3 ('Dangling-deletion recall eval passes') provides no test corpus, no recall threshold, and no proving command — 'passes' is undefined and the criterion is not measurable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `tests/fixtures/deletion_impact/` directory with at least 4 synthetic diff cases and update AC3 to: 'pytest tests/test_deletion_impact_overlay.py passes, with recall ≥ 1.0 on the fixture set and zero false positives on the rename-with-update case.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlay_triggers` (steps.py) was considered for extension (e.g. a `diff_regex_triggers` input that operates on diff-line text) and rejected — if rejected, document the reason; if not considered, evaluate whether the new `registry.py` content-trigger seam is justified vs. extending the existing mechanism, given it has exactly one current consumer.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'content-triggered overlay' as a new general seam in `registry.glob_triggered_overlays` (registry.py) with exactly one concrete consumer (the deletion-impact overlay), while the codebase already contains `overlay_triggers` (steps.py:115-173) — a general workflow step that performs text/keyword-based boolean trigger computation with an established test suite (test_overlay_triggers.py, 8 tests) and existing call-sites. The new seam appears to rebuild the text-matching trigger pattern in a code-review-specific module rather than extending or specializing the existing mechanism. The plan should confirm whether (a) extending `overlay_triggers` with a regex/diff-line predicate mode was considered and rejected (with a stated reason), or (b) the new seam in registry.py is intentionally code-review-specific (diff-text unavailable to the generic workflow step layer) and will have ≥2 additional planned consumers beyond deletion-impact.
findings.location: Scope: `registry.py (trigger seam)` / What section: 'content-triggered overlays'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the polyglot trigger regex (or pattern per language) in the plan, including which languages are in scope and example lines that must/must not match.
findings.finding: The 'polyglot regex over minus lines' that triggers the deletion-impact overlay is not specified — the executor must independently design the regex pattern, which is a real design choice deferred to implementation.
findings.location: What section / Acceptance Criteria #2

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete tool or API the deletion-impact overlay will use for reverse-reference lookup (e.g., grep-over-diff-context, ctags index + grep, opengrep structural pattern, or Pass-2 grep-the-specific-caller), and confirm that tool is available in the overlay's agentic execution context.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the agentic deletion-impact overlay will 'search for surviving callers/references of deleted/renamed/re-signatured symbols,' but does not specify what concrete tools or APIs enable this reverse-reference lookup. The plan itself acknowledges the grounding oracle has no reverse-reference lookup, leaving the capability of the agentic overlay unverified.
findings.location: What section: 'triggered when the diff removes a def/class/signature (polyglot regex over '-' lines); searches for surviving callers/references of deleted/renamed/re-signatured symbols and flags dangling ones.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the caller-discovery mechanism in the deletion-impact overlay design: confirm the agentic overlay will use file-read/grep tool calls to search for usages of each deleted symbol, and note this in the prompt spec (code-review-deletion-impact.md) or the Scope section.
findings.finding: The deletion-impact overlay asserts it 'searches for surviving callers/references of deleted/renamed/re-signatured symbols' but never specifies the mechanism by which the agentic overlay locates callers — the grounding oracle explicitly lacks reverse-reference lookup, and the plan does not state whether the LLM agent will use file-read/grep tool calls, ctags forward lookups per deleted name, or another method. This leaves the core value-delivery step (caller discovery) as an unverified capability.
findings.location: ## What — deletion-impact overlay description; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-deletion-impact.md (named deliverable, does not yet exist)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the ## What section: 'The agentic overlay discovers callers by grep-searching the live tree for each extracted symbol name using its file-read tools (the standard agentic toolset already granted to overlays), scoped to the surviving (non-deleted) files.' This grounds the capability claim in the existing agentic infrastructure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `code-review-deletion-impact.md` uses `output_schema: code_review_findings` (reusing the existing overlay contract), and define the fallback behavior when the agentic caller-search sub-step errors or returns no output (e.g., emit a coverage-gap note via `coverage_gap_note()` analogous to `base_failure_result`).
findings.finding: The deletion-impact overlay prompt (code-review-deletion-impact.md) is named in Scope but its output schema, the agentic tool calls it may make (for one-hop caller search), and the fallback when those tool calls fail/time-out are all unspecified. Every existing overlay finder uses `output_schema: code_review_findings` (the `findings_model()` contract); the deletion-impact overlay is 'agentic, findings-only' which implies the same schema, but the plan does not state this, and the one-hop LLM search sub-step has no error/abstain fallback unlike the base reviewer's `base_failure_result()`.
findings.location: What section ('agentic, findings-only, one-hop') / Scope ('src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-deletion-impact.md') / src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py:findings_model

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete mechanism for caller/reference lookup (e.g., 'grep -rn over the surviving diff context', 'tree-sitter AST query', or 'LLM sub-prompt with codebase context') and its scope boundaries.
findings.finding: The 'one-hop' caller-search mechanism is not specified — the plan mentions searching for 'surviving callers/references' but does not state whether this uses grep, AST analysis, or an LLM, leaving a real design choice to the executor.
findings.location: What section — 'searches for surviving callers/references of deleted/renamed/re-signatured symbols'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises the deletion-impact overlay end-to-end against the live model (or documents a rationale and names the child task that will do so), e.g., 'Running the review pipeline on a synthetic diff with a deleted function produces at least one deletion-impact advisory finding from the LLM overlay.'
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the live agentic LLM overlay end-to-end (i.e., a real diff triggering the overlay and producing findings via the actual model), meaning the new code path could be 'complete' with only offline/mocked tests that never invoke the LLM.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling for the deletion-impact LLM call: at minimum a timeout, a catch-and-log path that emits an advisory-skipped notice rather than raising, and (optionally) a retry budget.
findings.finding: The plan adds an LLM overlay call (an external integration failure point) with no stated error-handling strategy: no retry, no timeout, no graceful-degradation path if the LLM is unavailable or returns a malformed response.
findings.location: What section — 'New deletion-impact overlay (agentic, findings-only, one-hop)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full lifecycle for content-trigger registry entries: who registers them and when (e.g., config parse at process start), whether they are mutable after init, and how stale entries are cleaned up if the overlay config is removed.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new shared registry state (content-trigger predicates alongside `glob_triggered_overlays`) but does not specify its CREATE/UPDATE/RETIRE lifecycle: which component registers these entries, at what point in the process lifecycle, whether updates are possible after initialization, or whether stale/removed entries are cleaned up.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py (trigger seam); Why/What sections
findings.suggested_fix: Confirm that content-trigger predicates are registered exactly once at config-parse time (same lifecycle as glob triggers), are read-only at runtime, and that removing an entry from gates/code-review.yaml automatically removes it from the registry on next load — document this explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why a generalized content-trigger seam is needed rather than wiring the deletion-predicate directly into `overlay_union` (or as a special case in `glob_triggered_overlays`); cite at least one additional content-trigger use case that motivates the abstraction, or scope the implementation to a direct/minimal hook.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a generalized content-trigger seam ('registry.glob_triggered_overlays seam') with exactly one current consumer (the deletion-impact overlay) and no stated second or third use case to justify the abstraction. This risks premature generalization over a simpler direct wiring of the deletion predicate into overlay_union.
findings.location: What / Scope section: 'New capability: content-triggered overlays — a deterministic diff-content predicate that activates an overlay, alongside today's path-glob triggers (registry.glob_triggered_overlays seam)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) name a second concrete content-trigger use case that would use this seam (e.g. a 'large-diff' trigger, a 'binary-file' trigger) to satisfy Rule-of-Three, or (b) scope the implementation as a direct predicate parameter to overlay_union rather than a new named seam, and defer the generalization until a second consumer appears.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured log line (trigger fired/not-fired + matched symbol) and a counter metric (e.g. deletion_impact_overlay_triggered_total) so the overlay's behavior is observable in production.
findings.finding: New failure points (content-trigger firing, LLM overlay invocation, caller-search results) are not instrumented with any logging, metric, or trace, leaving operators unable to diagnose why the overlay did or did not fire.
findings.location: Scope section and Acceptance Criteria — no observability mention

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the content-trigger seam is justified now (e.g., note any other planned/imminent content-triggered overlays that would use it), or explicitly document that it is a deliberate extension of the existing glob-trigger pattern rather than a standalone new abstraction.
findings.finding: The content-triggered overlay seam is a new abstraction with exactly one current consumer (the deletion-impact overlay). The Rule-of-Three guideline in G5 requires ≥3 real call-sites to justify a new abstraction; a single call-site is a YAGNI signal worth acknowledging, even though the low cost of symmetry with the existing glob-trigger pattern mitigates risk.
findings.location: Scope / registry.py trigger seam; AC: 'Content-trigger seam added and unit-tested'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief comment in the plan (or in registry.py) noting that content-trigger is an extension of the existing glob-trigger seam, and name at least one other candidate overlay (e.g., a future 'large-file-churn' overlay) to justify the general mechanism — or collapse the seam into the deletion-impact overlay itself and generalise only when a second consumer arrives.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
